You instantly become compute bound. What they do is they pack the ceiling of 128 [...] Mini Max sparse attention by the team at Mini Max.. Right. So the core insight here for you listening is that they build a block wise, sparse attention mechanism directly on top of grouped query attention or GQA. And they use this really lightweight, entirely exp- free index branch to retrieve only the top K key value blocks per GQA group. Yeah. And then the main branch executes a KV outer sparse attention kernel. Exactly. And the hardware level wall clock speedups are just staggering. results.title: Thoughts on MiniMax M3's MSA (MiniMax Sparse Attention)?
results.content:
How it works is they partition the KV cache into blocks and use a KV-outer-gather-Q approach instead of the usual query-centric sparse attention. Each block gets read once, memory access stays contiguous. They claim it matches full attention on most capabilities across ablations which if true is the important part - sparse attention that degrades quality isn't useful. [...] Every chinese model moving to sparse attention is kinda crazy - they're going after cheap tokens and doing so with hardware that doesn't need to be competitive with nvidia

index plus sparse branch design is the interesting bit. instead of attending to everything it scores blocks and only does full attention on the top ones, thats how you get the big long context speedup without the usual quality hit. reminds me of the nsa direction deepseek published but more pared down. waiting on the weights and the tech report to see how it trains, but on the api the long context coherence is already better than i expected.

 Reply  Share [...] The main thing: they built a new sparse attention mechanism called MSA (MiniMax Sparse Attention) specifically to make 1M token context viable without the usual quadratic compute explosion. The numbers they're claiming are wild if accurate - 1/20th the per-token compute of their previous gen at 1M context, 9x faster prefilling, 15x faster decoding. For local inference that matters a lot more than it does for API usage since you're actually the one paying the VRAM and compute bill.

results.content:
worth comparing. Long context is not a memory feature by itself; it is a retrieval problem under brutal latency constraints, where the model must decide what deserves bandwidth at the moment of use. MiniMax Sparse Attention is compelling because it moves that decision into the architecture, trains the selector against the model’s own attention patterns. ---- Link – arxiv. org/abs/2606.13392 Title: "MiniMax Sparse Attention" [...] 8:39 PM · Jun 15, 20266.3KViews

Restructuring memory access patterns at the operator level rather than just sparsifying the attention matrix is the right architectural intuition. Most sparse attention methods still hit the same memory bottleneck because the access pattern remains random

# People also ask about section

Details on MiniMax MSA and context windows [...] # MiniMax dropped a new attention architecture. [N] : r/MachineLearning
Skip to main contentMiniMax dropped a new attention architecture. [N] : r/MachineLearning

Open menu Open navigation, bypassing standard quadratic complexity by completely restructuring the memory access patterns at the operator level.

Instead of relying on typical sparse approximations that degrade recall, MSA utilizes a clean "_KV outer gather Q_" approach.

By treating KV blocks as the outer loop to aggregate hit queries, hardware memory reads remain strictly contiguous, and each block is fetched exactly once.

The low-level performance gains are interesting:

→ 4× faster execution speed compared to Flash-Sparse-Attention.

results.title: MiniMax teases M3 model with new sparse attention mechanism ...
results.content:
In large language models, "quadratic scaling" refers to the computationally expensive reality of standard full attention mechanisms, where every token in a sequence must mathematically connect to every other token. To use a real-world analogy, it is akin to attending a networking event and being forced to have a deep conversation with every single person in the room while simultaneously monitoring all other ongoing conversations.

While this approach yields incredibly thorough context, the processing power and memory required explode at the square of the input length, creating a severe hardware bottleneck as models attempt to ingest hundreds of thousands of words.

## The problem with sub-quadratic scaling [...] The empirical results were definitive: at a larger scale, linear and windowed attention variants exhibited severe reasoning deficits.

On evaluations exceeding 32K context windows, SWA variants performed significantly worse than full attention, dropping from a baseline score of 90.0 to 72.0 on the RULER 128K complex word extraction task.

Sub-quadratic configurations proved prone to memory-bound constraints during training, lacked native prefix caching support, and failed to smoothly align with Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) modules used for speculative decoding. Full attention was deemed necessary to preserve multi-hop reasoning capability. [...] ## The attention dilemma

The core technical architecture of the M2 series relies on a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decoder-only Transformer layout used by numerous other state-of-the-art LLMs.

The foundational backbone houses 229.9 billion total parameters, yet maintains a remarkably lean operational footprint by activating just 9.8 billion parameters per token across 256 fine-grained experts.

To optimize routing and avoid standard load-balancing issues, however, MiniMax implemented sigmoid gating paired with learnable, expert-specific bias terms, heavily reducing reliance on restrictive auxiliary losses.

The most definitive engineering decision documented in the M2 paper was the strict adherence to full multi-head attention with Grouped Query Attention (GQA) across all 62 layers.
